# Benchmark Experiments {#sec-benchmarks}

## Experimental protocol

CA is compared against four widely used metaheuristics: the real-coded Genetic Algorithm (GA) with tournament selection, BLX-$\alpha$ crossover, Gaussian mutation, and elitism [@holland1975; @goldberg1989]; Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) with linearly decreasing inertia [@kennedy1995]; Simulated Annealing (SA) [@kirkpatrick1983] granted a population-equivalent evaluation budget; and the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) [@mirjalili2014gwo].

The test suite comprises six standard functions spanning unimodal, ill-conditioned, and highly multimodal landscapes:

| Function | Type | Search domain | $f^\*$ |
|---|---|---|---:|
| F1 Sphere | Unimodal | $[-100, 100]^{30}$ | 0 |
| F2 Rosenbrock | Unimodal, ill-conditioned valley | $[-30, 30]^{30}$ | 0 |
| F3 Rastrigin | Multimodal | $[-5.12, 5.12]^{30}$ | 0 |
| F4 Griewank | Multimodal | $[-600, 600]^{30}$ | 0 |
| F5 Ackley | Multimodal | $[-32, 32]^{30}$ | 0 |
| F6 Schwefel 2.22 | Unimodal, non-separable norm | $[-10, 10]^{30}$ | 0 |

: Benchmark functions ($D = 30$). {#tbl-suite}

All algorithms use a population of $N = 30$, $T = 500$ iterations, and therefore an identical budget of $15{,}000$ function evaluations per run (CA's en-passant and castling probes are included in its accounting by design of the shared interface). Each algorithm–function pair is repeated for $30$ independent runs with distinct seeds. No per-problem parameter tuning was performed for any algorithm, including CA.

Statistical analysis follows @derrac2011: one-way ANOVA to establish that the algorithm factor matters, and pairwise two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum tests (CA versus each competitor) at $\alpha = 0.05$, which do not assume normality of final-error distributions.

## Descriptive results

{{< include ../results/table_stats.md >}}

: Mean, standard deviation, best, and worst final objective values over 30 runs (best mean per function in bold). {#tbl-stats tbl-colwidths="[18,12,18,18,17,17]"}

## Convergence behavior

@fig-convergence shows the median best-so-far curves. Three patterns are visible. First, CA descends steadily throughout the run on F1, F4, and F6: the en-passant success rule keeps producing improvements deep into the endgame, where GA and PSO have long stagnated. Second, on the ill-conditioned Rosenbrock valley (F2) all population methods slow dramatically, with CA and GA tracking each other closely. Third, GWO exhibits its well-documented strength on this classical suite, converging fastest and deepest on F1, F5, and F6.

![Median convergence curves (30 runs, semi-log scale).](../figures/convergence_all.png){#fig-convergence fig-align="center" width="100%"}

![Distribution of final objective values over 30 runs.](../figures/boxplots_all.png){#fig-boxplots fig-align="center" width="100%"}

## Statistical tests

One-way ANOVA confirms highly significant between-algorithm differences on every function:

{{< include ../results/table_anova.md >}}

: One-way ANOVA over the five algorithms, per function. {#tbl-anova}

Pairwise Wilcoxon rank-sum tests give the sharper picture:

{{< include ../results/table_wilcoxon.md >}}

: Wilcoxon rank-sum tests, CA versus each competitor ($\alpha = 0.05$). {#tbl-wilcoxon}

## Discussion

The evidence supports a differentiated, honest reading:

- **CA versus GA, PSO, and SA.** CA is significantly better than PSO and SA on *all six* functions, and significantly better than GA on four of six (Sphere, Griewank, Schwefel 2.22 by several orders of magnitude in mean error; Ackley favors GA on the mean owing to a few CA runs trapped on a plateau, although CA's median error, $\sim 10^{-3}$, is an order of magnitude below GA's). On Rosenbrock and Rastrigin, CA and GA are statistically indistinguishable.
- **CA versus GWO.** GWO is significantly better on all six classical benchmarks, consistent with its known aggressive exploitation on this suite [@mirjalili2014gwo]. We report this plainly: on standard unconstrained test functions, CA does not overtake GWO.
- **No free lunch.** These results are exactly the situation anticipated by @wolpert1997: rankings are problem-dependent. The practical question for a transportation engineer is not whether CA wins on Sphere, but whether it is competitive on structured engineering problems — the subject of @sec-transport, where the ranking changes and CA matches or exceeds all competitors, including GWO.

In summary, CA clearly dominates three of the four established baselines on the majority of the suite while remaining behind GWO on classical benchmarks — a profile typical of a young algorithm with a distinctive inductive bias rather than a tuned benchmark specialist.
